They connect specific moments in the video to the sexting ('at 2:41 I was thinking about what you said about…'). Fantasies are ranked from least to most explicit; each PPV in the loop uses the next fantasy up the ladder. This gives chatters an inexhaustible, varied pitch mechanism that doesn't repeat.

sources.quote: an alternative to that is sexting as if you were opening up about a fantasy that the creator has been having
you keep talking about the fantasy until they're clearly into it, and then you link that to the next pay-per-view saying something like, I made this video thinking about doing that for the first time

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatting scripts or sexting frameworks exist at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is a build-toward tactic for the in-house chat team and AI chatting project. The AI chatting project in particular would need this fantasy-ladder logic codified as a sequencing rule.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the in-house chat roadmap, build a 'fantasy library' per creator (ranked low-to-high explicitness) that chatters and eventually AI chatters draw from sequentially per subscriber. Document the indirect sexting structure as a script template: embarrassed open → full fantasy build → PPV link with timestamped personal connection. This is especially relevant for the AI chatting project where consistent, structured dialogue trees are required.

new_findings.topic: Objection handling — add value before discounting
new_findings.title: When a subscriber objects to price, add more content first; only discount by removing content, not by lowering the price

variants.claim: Reflexively discounting when a subscriber says 'too expensive' trains them to always haggle; instead, offer more content at the same price first, and if a discount is unavoidable, remove content from the bundle rather than simply lowering the stated price.
variants.what_they_do: Rule 1 — Value-add first: if a sub resists price, add a small extra (bonus clip, picture set) and make it a huge deal, framing it as a personal favor. This gets full price paid and doesn't establish a discount expectation. Rule 2 — Renegotiation discount: if discounting is unavoidable, pre-load the PPV with a few extra pictures/clips by default. When the sub asks for less, remove those extras and drop the price — so the price reduction is tied to a concrete content reduction, not arbitrary. This preserves the perception that prices are meaningful. Rule 3 — Underpriced sub recovery: if a sub accepts a dropped price too readily, immediately propose a larger bundle ('I had an idea — I want to throw in extra stuff') to recapture upside.

sources.quote: it is a better first response to try and give them something small to sweeten the deal, and you get paid the full amount as opposed to just discounting
if they ask for a discount, you can take that stuff out and then drop the price, so you've now renegotiated

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal objection-handling framework exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project, where objection-handling logic needs to be systematized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'add value before discounting, renegotiate via content removal' a non-negotiable chatter policy in the future in-house SOP. For the AI chatting project, encode this as a decision tree: objection detected → attempt value-add response → if second objection → remove content and lower price. Also document the underpriced-sub recovery move as a standard upsell trigger.

new_findings.topic: Creator quality bar — A-tier targeting vs. settling
new_findings.title: Hold out for A-tier creators; 3 months with a weak creator costs more than 3 months outreaching

variants.claim: Spending months outreaching for a single high-quality creator yields more revenue and less wasted effort than onboarding an easy-to-sign but unmotivated creator.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin explicitly refuses to sign creators who don't film on time, lack motivation, or don't meet appearance standards. He targets creators with 5k–50k followers who already have or look suitable for OnlyFans. He argues a bad 3-month engagement versus a 3-month search for an A-tier creator is a false economy because agency leverage multiplies creator quality.

sources.quote: if you were to spend those same exact 3 months outreaching and then sign your A tier creator, you can make not only $10,000, $20,000 a month
the better your creator is, the more your work will show for it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented creator qualification criteria or scoring system (§6 GAP). Onboarding form captures name, email, commission %, state — but no quality or potential assessment.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a written creator qualification rubric (follower range, content consistency, responsiveness, existing OF presence, appearance tier) to be applied before any sales call is booked. Add a qualification score field to the Creators Airtable table so onboarding decisions are data-driven, not intuition-based.

new_findings.topic: Pre-signing ops stack — Notion, Discord, Telegram
new_findings.title: Stand up the full ops stack (Notion + Discord + Telegram) before signing creator #1

variants.claim: Agency infrastructure — dashboards, chatter management hub, and content distribution channels — must be fully configured before onboarding the first creator so signing can be fast and error-free.
variants.what_they_do: Griffin uses: Notion for the agency dashboard and KPI tracking; Discord as the chatter management server (chatters plugged into one repeatable process as they're hired); Telegram for content distribution. All three are set up in the foundation phase, before any outreach begins.

sources.quote: get your notion dashboard set up, your team management, your Discord hub for chatting, your Telegram for content distribution. Everything needs to be set up before even signing a creator
manage these chatters in Discord from day one. That way when you hire more and more, it's just plugging them into the same process

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses Airtable (HQ + Content Ops), Telegram (routing to Amin for posting), and Publer for scheduling (§5). Palm does NOT use Notion or Discord; chatter management is outsourced externally rather than managed in a Discord hub (§8). The stack overlap on Telegram is real, but Discord-based chatter management is absent.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap, §8), evaluate Discord as the coordination layer for chatters rather than building a bespoke system. It's the consistent standard across competitor agencies and would minimize onboarding friction when hiring chatters. For now, document the existing Airtable + Telegram + Publer stack as the official SOP so new hires have a clear baseline.

new_findings.topic: PPV ladder and first-PPV price anchoring
new_findings.title: Price first PPV at $15–$25 and use a 'Yes Train' commitment loop to pre-sell before sending

variants.claim: A soft verbal commitment ('yes train') before sending the first PPV — combined with a $15–$25 price point and partial-nudity content — significantly increases buy-through rates.
variants.what_they_do: Before sending any PPV, the chatter runs a 'yes train': a question that sounds like consent ('if I do this, do you promise you'll go easy on me?') but functions as a purchase pre-commitment. When the fan says yes, he feels psychologically obligated to buy the PPV that follows. First PPV is priced $15–$25 (higher if the model is positioned as premium; lower if marketed with explicit free content). Content is partial nudity only (top off). Subsequent PPVs escalate: bottoms off → full nude → solo play. Between each PPV, the chatter rebuilds arousal with free teasers and descriptive text before the next ask. The session ends when the fan signals spending fatigue — at which point no further PPV is pushed.

sources.quote: the yes train is basically getting a confirmation, getting a yes from him so that when later you send the PPV, he feels obliged to buy it because he already said yes
first PPV you will price $15 to $25 this depends on your model where you market her and how you market her

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model or ladder (§7 GAP — noted as '#1 creator concern'). This is a concrete, tested pricing structure from a $100k/month operation. The 'yes train' pre-sell mechanic is also absent from any internal SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this as a starting-point PPV ladder for the in-house chat team: first PPV $15–$25 (partial nudity), second PPV (full nudity) $20–$35, third+ (solo play) $25–$50+. Adjust per creator tier. Document the 'yes train' as a mandatory pre-PPV step in the chat SOP. Also use this ladder to inform the pricing section (§7) — this is the most concrete competitor data Palm has on PPV structure.

new_findings.title: Send a 'gratification video' at session end to convert spent fans into loyal returning subscribers

variants.claim: Sending a warm, smiling 'thank you' video after a fan's spending session — when he is emotionally most vulnerable — builds the relationship bond that drives repeat monetization rather than churn.
variants.what_they_do: When a fan signals spending fatigue, the chatter stops pushing PPVs entirely. Instead, the model sends a pre-filmed 'gratification video': short clip of her smiling, saying she had a great time, expressing genuine-seeming warmth. The chatter then attempts to extend the chat slightly rather than letting the fan leave immediately. The logic: the fan is emotionally open at this moment; a positive close imprints a good memory that makes him return for the full PPV ladder again next session.

sources.quote: this is just a simple video of the model saying thank you and that she had a really good time to the camera being happy smiling. So you make the fan feel good. This is where they're most vulnerable to relationship building
you repeat this process until the fan can't spend anymore. When the fan can't spend anymore, you don't want to force any more sales because this will just ruin the whole relationship

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has partial fan retention infrastructure (whale hunting, Fan Tracker — §9) but no documented post-session strategy or churn prevention tactic at the individual fan level. This is a concrete retention mechanic that complements the existing Fan Tracker CRM.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add 'gratification close' as a required final step in the in-house chat SOP. Pre-film one gratification video per creator per content set. In the Fan Tracker (§9), add a 'last gratification sent' timestamp field to track recency and ensure it's deployed consistently. Consider this the retention equivalent of a post-purchase email — systematize it the same way.

new_findings.title: Build DM scripts empirically: test freestyle lines, promote high-performing ones into the canonical script

variants.claim: The most effective chat scripts are built bottom-up from live testing — chatters freestyle, track what gets positive responses and high PPV buy rates, and promote those lines/setups into the official script iteratively.
variants.what_they_do: The agency does not hand chatters a finished script to copy-paste. They provide the structural framework (rapport → passive tease → yes train → PPV ladder → gratification close) and then chatters freestyle within it. When a specific line or PPV setup consistently produces positive fan responses or high buy rates, it gets promoted into the agency's canonical script. The script is therefore a living document refined by actual conversion data.

sources.quote: If we see that a certain sentence has positive feedback from most subscribers, we will use it in the script. If a PPV has a high buy rate with a certain PPV setup, we will use it in the script
how we create scripts in our agency is we get on the page, we start chatting and see what works

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and no internal script system exists. For the AI chatting roadmap project specifically, this empirical methodology is directly applicable — Palm could A/B test AI chat lines against fan response data and auto-promote winning variants. This also aligns with Palm's existing data-driven content ideation approach (§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team and AI chatting system, implement a structured script iteration process: (1) define the structural framework as non-negotiable, (2) allow freestyle within phases, (3) require chatters to flag high-performing lines with a simple tagging system (Airtable or Telegram), (4) conduct weekly script review to promote winners. For AI chatting, this maps to prompt versioning with conversion tracking — build that feedback loop into the architecture from day one.

new_findings.title: Replace generic greetings with open-ended, personalized openers

variants.claim: Using open-ended, flirty questions instead of generic greetings like 'hey' or 'how are you' dramatically increases subscriber engagement.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to never open with 'hello' or 'how are you'; instead they ask about the fan's day in a flirty, playful way, reference previous conversations ('you made me blush'), and use the subscriber's name periodically to create intimacy. Example opener: 'Hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush.'

sources.quote: use open-ended questions instead of just typing hello or greetings ask engaging questions avoid asking how or you because it is so boring
instead of copy pasting a generic hey try messaging hey handsome I was just thinking about our last conversation you made me blush

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is currently outsourced to an external chat team (§8); no internal SOP or scripted opener standards exist. This is directly relevant build-toward material for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat SOP, codify a banned-phrases list ('hey', 'how are you', 'okay') and a required-opener format: fan name + callback to prior interaction + flirty open-ended question. Embed this in chatter onboarding scripts.

new_findings.topic: Urgency and exclusivity framing for PPV
new_findings.title: Apply time-sensitive + VIP-exclusivity framing to every PPV offer

variants.claim: Framing PPV offers as time-limited and personally exclusive ('only for you') reliably increases conversion rates.
variants.what_they_do: Two distinct psychological levers are scripted: (1) urgency — 'only available for a few hours'; (2) exclusivity — 'I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special.' Both are delivered conversationally so they feel organic, not like a sales blast.

sources.quote: make the offer feel exclusive and time sensitive example I have something special for you but it's only available for a few hours
I don't usually send this to just anyone but you're special I love talking to you

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing or sales-framing baseline exists at Palm (§7 GAP). The external chat team may or may not use these frames; Palm has no oversight of that language.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include both urgency and exclusivity language templates in the PPV script library Palm will build for its in-house chat team. Also share these frames with the current outsourced team as a performance-improvement brief. Track conversion rate before/after to validate.

new_findings.title: Send delayed, personalized one-liners as first message — never paragraphs or PPVs

variants.claim: A short, delayed welcome message (3 min after sub) that references the fan's profile bio/name outperforms generic paragraphs because it signals authenticity and avoids setting a low price anchor.
variants.what_they_do: Use Creator Hero's delayed-message feature to send a simple 'Hi' or name-based joke 3 minutes after subscription. If no reply, follow up with a profile-specific observation (name joke, bio reference) to signal a real person is behind the account. Never send a PPV or long paragraph as the first message.

sources.quote: It's better to just say, 'Hi, hello, what's up?' than to write him a whole paragraph as a welcoming message
you can look at his profile, his bio, his name, and you can just say something about his name, make a joke

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP or scripts. There is no documented welcome-message protocol. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, codify: (1) use delayed send ~3 min post-sub, (2) open with ≤1 short line referencing fan's name/bio, (3) do not price-anchor or send PPV until rapport is established. Document this as Step 1 of the chatter onboarding script.

new_findings.title: Gauge spending power through rapport questions before setting any PPV price

variants.claim: Talking to a fan before quoting any price lets chatters learn his job/lifestyle and set a higher first PPV price ($25 vs $10) because no anchor was established early.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters ask open questions about job and hobbies — not from a script, but organically — to extract spending-power signals. Light flirting and jokes are used; sexual escalation is avoided until the fan initiates. The chatter never names a price before this rapport window closes.

sources.quote: we do use scripts inside our agency, but we never have the rapport part scripted as it's really important to let the conversation run smoothly
you're also going to get a glimpse of his spending power. So for how much you can send him PPVs

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). Palm's whale-hunting module (§9) collects fan spend data post-hoc but there is no documented pre-sale rapport protocol that informs pricing in real time.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For the in-house chat SOP, add a mandatory rapport phase (est. 5–10 messages) before any PPV is sent. Train chatters to log job/lifestyle signals in fan notes and use them to bracket PPV price — e.g., blue-collar → $15–25, professional → $30–60. Tie this to the Fan Tracker CRM (§9).

new_findings.title: Run a repeating descriptive-text + free-teaser + PPV loop until fan 'taps out' — then stop selling

variants.claim: After the first PPV purchase, cycling between descriptive text exchange, free teasers, and the next PPV sell maximizes session revenue; stopping the moment the fan finishes or runs out of money protects the long-term relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Post-first-purchase loop: (1) ask 'what would you do now if you saw me like this?' to get descriptive text, (2) mirror his fantasy back, (3) insert free teaser (photo/voice/short clip — not enough to satisfy), (4) set up next PPV with yes-ladder, (5) sell. Repeat until fan orgasms or explicitly says no money. At that point, immediately pivot to relationship-building mode — pre-recorded 'had a great time' appreciation video, romantic post-session questions.

sources.quote: descriptive text, sound turned on, get descriptive text from him, make him imagine the scenario in his head, send free teasers, set up the next PPV, and sell the PPV
Sell until you can sell, right? Until he's tapped out or he comes.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline (§8, outsourced). This is the core revenue engine loop that Palm's outsourced team presumably runs but Palm has no documented version of.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document this loop as the core 'session playbook' in the in-house chatter training. Define a session KPI (revenue per session, PPVs per session) and track per chatter. Pre-record a library of appreciation/wind-down clips for each creator so chatters can deploy them instantly at session end.

new_findings.title: Five-tier chat priority queue: active sext session first, time-wasters last

variants.claim: Organizing the subscriber inbox into five explicit priority tiers ensures chatters always work highest-revenue conversations first and never waste time on non-spenders while whales wait.
variants.what_they_do: Priority order: (1) fans currently in a sext/purchase session, (2) fans being transitioned into a session (teasing phase), (3) new subscribers (unknown spend potential — test them), (4) known spenders/whales in relationship-building, (5) time-wasters — only addressed when inbox is empty. Agency adjusts exact ordering based on where their revenue concentrates.

sources.quote: number one in my opinion for everybody should be the ones that are currently in a sexting session
The last ones should be time wasters because they're just wasting our time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module (§9) and Fan Tracker CRM but no documented real-time chat prioritization queue. The outsourced chat team may have their own system, but Palm has no visibility into it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat team, implement this queue as a visible status layer in the Fan Tracker CRM. Consider surfacing active-session and new-subscriber flags in the whale-hunting dashboard (§9) so the chat manager can monitor live priority distribution across creators.

new_findings.title: Tag fans with emoji labels in their OF display name for instant at-a-glance segmentation

variants.claim: Adding emoji tags (whale emoji, feet emoji, etc.) directly to a fan's OnlyFans display name lets chatters instantly identify spend tier and kink preference without opening notes, critical when managing high inbox volume.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters edit the fan's name field in the OF inbox to prepend relevant emojis: 🐳 for whale, specific kink emojis (e.g., foot emoji for feet fetish). This means the chatter sees the tag the moment the chat appears in the list and can tailor the opening message without reading back through history.

sources.quote: you can put emojis in their name, right? In their OnlyFans name and you can quickly see what type of subscriber they are
a specific kink they might have if they're into feet. You can put a feet emoji in their name

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's Fan Tracker CRM (§9) stores per-fan data but requires opening a record. This emoji-in-name trick is a zero-friction, platform-native shortcut that works inside OF itself — Palm's CRM layer doesn't replicate this speed.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a Palm emoji taxonomy (tier: 🐳 whale / 💰 spender / ⏳ time-waster; kink tags) and add it to chatter onboarding training. Pair with Fan Tracker: emoji = quick-scan layer in OF; CRM = detailed notes. Low-cost, high-speed UX improvement for any in-house chatter.

new_findings.title: Use 50/50 split-payment to overcome custom content price objections without discounting

variants.claim: When a fan objects to a custom content price, offering 50% upfront and 50% on delivery preserves the full price while reducing the perceived financial risk — avoiding the margin hit of a discount.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter expresses genuine excitement about the custom idea, adds her own suggestions to raise perceived effort/value, then quotes a premium price. On price objection: offer to deliver 'today/in 30 minutes' (urgency) and propose 50% now, 50% after delivery. Only drop the price as a final fallback if the 50/50 offer also fails.

sources.quote: you can offer him 50/50. So he pays 50% before and 50% after
instead of dropping the price, you can tell him to pay half now, pay half later

palm_comparison.vs_us: No pricing or custom-content SOP at Palm (§7 gap, §8 outsourced). The 50/50 mechanic is a concrete objection-handling script Palm can directly adopt for both the in-house chat roadmap and any custom-content pricing guide.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add the 50/50 split-pay offer as a named objection-handling step in the chatter SOP, positioned before any price reduction. Also define a custom content pricing floor (e.g., 3–5× standard PPV price) so chatters know what 'premium' means before quoting. Document this in the pricing model (§7 gap).

new_findings.title: Log fan hobbies, fetishes, and job in subscriber notes to personalize DMs

variants.claim: Building rapport with subscribers requires learning what they are genuinely into (fetishes, hobbies, job) and recording those details in the subscriber's notes field to enable personalized follow-up.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters skip surface-level questions (age, location) and instead probe for fetishes, hobbies, and occupation. Every paying subscriber gets a notes entry with those details so any chatter picking up the conversation later has context. Non-spenders are deprioritized — no time is wasted building rapport with subs who have not spent money.

sources.quote: what are they into right maybe they what do they do for work what are their Hobbies obviously if we're talking about only fans specifically what are they into like what are their fetishes
you don't want to be able to report with people who are just going to waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is currently outsourced (§8). Palm does have a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9) that capture per-fan data, but no documented SOP for what chatters should log or how to use notes to personalize conversations. This tactic speaks directly to both the build-toward in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building the in-house chat SOP, include a mandatory 'fan profile' step: before sending a PPV or upsell, chatters must log fetish, hobby, and occupation for any subscriber who has spent money. Gate the fan profile requirement at first purchase, not first message, to avoid wasting effort on non-buyers. Align this with the existing Fan Tracker table (§9).

new_findings.topic: Creator quality filter — work ethic over aesthetics
new_findings.title: Prioritize creator work ethic over follower count or physical appearance when signing

variants.claim: A less conventionally attractive or lower-follower creator with strong work ethic will generate more revenue for the agency than a high-follower or high-appearance creator who is lazy.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly tells agency owners to drop creators who are inactive or unwilling to produce content, and to instead seek out creators who are reliable and motivated even if they lack a large existing audience or a '9/10' appearance. Frames this as a simple resource-allocation decision.

sources.quote: you can work for somebody who maybe isn't 9 out of ten or maybe doesn't have 100K followers or whatever but she has the work ethic and she's always there that's who you're going to make a lot of money with
get rid simple bro there's so many other clients out there don't waste your time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition section (§6) has no documented qualification criteria for creators. The onboarding form captures name, email, and commission % but no work-ethic or output-commitment signals. Palm likely selects creators informally today.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a work-ethic qualification gate to the creator onboarding wizard (§6): require a brief voice memo or video response where the creator describes their current content output cadence and confirms a minimum posting commitment. Weight this more heavily than follower count in the signing decision. Also formalize an offboarding trigger: if a creator misses X consecutive content deadlines, the relationship is reviewed.

new_findings.topic: Multi-platform traffic strategy and platform volatility
new_findings.title: Diversify traffic sources across TikTok, Instagram Reels, and Reddit bot services because platform effectiveness shifts month to month

variants.claim: No single platform is the permanent best traffic source — TikTok and Instagram Reels alternate in effectiveness over weeks, while Reddit bot services offer more stable, updatable automation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitors which platform is currently converting and shifts effort accordingly (e.g., moved from TikTok to IG Reels during a two-month TikTok slump, then back). Reddit bot/automation services are treated as a stable baseline because they can be updated when Reddit changes its algorithm or removes bots. Maintains awareness of Reddit API/bot policy changes.

sources.quote: there is no best it always varies Tick Tock for the last two weeks it is working but the last two months before that it was very very like it just genuinely it was just bad so Instagram reels were better so it always changes
things that work really really well at least for us that we found are like bot Services they don't really mess up and it's pretty easy to then update these bot Services as well

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic/distribution strategy is an explicit GAP (§5). Palm posts via Publer and Telegram but has no documented platform-rotation or bot-service layer. Palm's content engine (§1) is strong on ideation but silent on which platform gets what content when.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a lightweight 'traffic pulse' review into Palm's weekly ops cadence: each week, flag which platform (TikTok vs IG Reels vs Reddit) is producing the most OF link clicks per creator. Use this to dynamically weight posting effort. Separately, evaluate Reddit bot/automation services as a low-maintenance evergreen traffic channel and document Palm's position on their use given platform-risk considerations (§platform-risk).

new_findings.title: Trend-Jack Active X Formats Daily for Guaranteed High-Impression Posts

variants.claim: Hopping on a trending X format with high-quality content almost guarantees 50K–200K+ impressions per post, far outperforming Instagram trend participation.
variants.what_they_do: Monitor X daily for trending formats in the creator's niche. As soon as a format gains traction (e.g., 'Hey Grok, put me in a bikini' AI image trend in Jan 2026), immediately produce a post in that format with high-quality creative and post it. Don't wait for certainty — first 5,000–10,000 participants get disproportionate reach. Historical trend formats used: POV/relatable scenery, 'Which one would you pick' poll + photos, 'Rate my outfit 1–10' mirror pic, AI-generated content prompts, 'This or that' polls. Cadence: check competitor/big accounts daily; if a format is getting traction, copy it fast.

sources.quote: Trendjacking. This is the cheat code. On Twitter, trends are everything. If you hop on a trend correctly and at the right time, you're guaranteed to blow up.
If you're one of the first 5,000 people, 10,000 people to hop on a trend and do it correctly with high quality, guaranteed to blow up.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is already data-driven and scrapes viral Reels from IG inspo accounts — this is exactly the same logic applied to X. Palm's system could theoretically be extended to scrape X trending formats. Currently Palm has no X monitoring in the Apify/inspo pipeline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add X trend monitoring to the content pipeline: include 5–10 X creator accounts in the Apify scrape or add a manual daily X check as a VA task. When a trending format is identified, create an X-specific task card in Airtable so the creator can produce the asset same-day. Mirror Palm's existing inspo-promote-film loop for X.

new_findings.title: Group-chat situational awareness test to screen for sales instinct before any live interview

variants.claim: A 10-question situational/emotional intelligence test sent to all shortlisted applicants in a single group chat efficiently identifies candidates with natural sales instinct without requiring individual calls.
variants.what_they_do: Create a group chat (WhatsApp or Telegram) with all applicants who passed Stage 1. Send a Google Docs with 10 situational questions designed to reveal emotional intelligence and objection-handling instinct — not chatting skill. Example: 'You own a vegan restaurant and someone asks for steak — what do you say?' The 'right' answer reframes the objection and redirects to an alternative rather than refusing. This filters out poor communicators before investing time in live interviews.

sources.quote: create a group chat with all of the applicants and you're going to give them their first test... a situational awareness test
you are the owner of a vegan restaurant and someone comes in asking for real meat, for a steak. What would you tell that person?

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter screening baseline (§8 outsourced). This is build-toward for the in-house chat team. Palm's current external chat team presumably handles their own quality — Palm has no visibility into how those chatters were vetted.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a Palm-specific situational awareness test (10 questions) as Stage 2 of the future chatter hiring pipeline. Frame questions around sales redirection rather than OF-specific knowledge. Use a group chat delivery mechanism to batch-process applicants efficiently.

new_findings.title: 90-min Loom training video with embedded accountability checkpoints to filter non-completers

variants.claim: Embedding hidden checkpoints (emoji signals, mid-video questions) inside a mandatory 90-minute onboarding video filters out candidates who won't follow through — before they ever touch a live fan account.
variants.what_they_do: After passing all screening stages, selected candidates receive a 90+ minute video covering: how OnlyFans works, how the CRM (Creator Hero) works, scripts, hotkeys, and job structure. The video includes 2–3 embedded accountability checks — e.g., 'send me a cherry emoji if you watched to here' or answer specific questions after viewing. Anyone who fails to complete or respond is cut. Rationale: if a chatter won't watch a Loom on Day 1, they won't perform reliably in month 2.

sources.quote: send them like a 90-minute plus video explaining the entire job... include a couple of accountability checks
if someone isn't even watching a Loom video you sent them at their first day on the job, you can imagine how he's going to work in a month from now

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatter onboarding (§8). This tactic is directly relevant to the AI chatting roadmap project and in-house chat team buildout. Palm already uses Loom-style documentation in other workflows — this pattern is familiar.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design Palm's future chatter onboarding as a video-first, checkpoint-gated flow. Use a single 60–90 min Loom (or similar) covering OF mechanics, Palm's CRM/tools, scripts, and KPIs, with 2–3 embedded compliance signals. Treat failure to complete as an automatic disqualification.

new_findings.title: Test 3–5 branding angles simultaneously in first two weeks; double down on data winner

variants.claim: Running multiple branding angles in parallel in the first two weeks (different captions, framings, positioning — same creator, same account) generates real performance data fast enough to commit to a winner before competitors even finish their first test.
variants.what_they_do: In weeks 1–2 they ran three branding angles simultaneously on the same account (Swedish girl, Ukrainian girl, American girl in Dubai) — same creator, same videos, different captions + framing + marketing positioning. Ukrainian angle got 2.4× the views of the other two combined. They killed the losing angles within two weeks and rebuilt all downstream systems (chatting scripts, DM persona voice, mass messages) to match the winning brand identity within the same week — not a month later.

sources.quote: in the first two weeks, we ran three branding angles through trials. We had a Swedish girl, a Ukrainian girl, and the American girl living in Dubai.
the back end just adds on top of it but fast…same week this shouldn't take you a month or two

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) uses inspo data to decide what to film but has no documented mechanism for parallel branding trials. Palm's onboarding doesn't include a defined brand-testing phase. This is a high-signal gap: Palm could run branding trials using its existing scheduling infrastructure (Publer) without new tooling.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'branding trial phase' (days 1–14) to Palm's creator onboarding: define 3 angle hypotheses based on inspo analysis, produce a small batch of content per angle, post in rotation, and set a data gate at day 14 (views, comments, shares, profile visits). Build the winning angle's identity into an Airtable 'Creator Brand Card' that downstream systems (chatting scripts, mass message tone, PPV captions) reference. Target: 3–5 branding tests per creator per quarter.

new_findings.topic: Creator relationship management and transparency as retention
new_findings.title: Radical transparency with creators (admit failures, show money flow) earns long-term loyalty

variants.claim: Being fully transparent with models about where their money goes, admitting when you're failing and why, and framing everything around their long-term income sustainability earns far more loyalty than over-promising.
variants.what_they_do: He tells creators explicitly where agency fees go and how ad spend is allocated. When metrics underperform, he proactively explains why rather than going silent. When a creator wants to leave, he releases them without conflict and often admits his own faults. He reports that 8 out of 10 creators who leave return. He pitches creators with numbers and technical detail — no yachts, no content houses, no collab promises.

sources.quote: being fully transparent is very important to me. If I'm failing at something, I will tell them I'm failing at this thing. Now I'll tell them why I'm failing at it
if you want to leave and go somewhere else, I'm not going to fight you... eight out of 10 times they'll revisit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has weekly invoicing (§7) but no documented creator success cadence, QBR process, or retention strategy (§9 GAP). The specific mechanics here — proactive failure disclosure, no-fight exits, reinvestment transparency — are directly adoptable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Implement a monthly creator check-in cadence for Palm where the manager shares: (1) revenue trend vs. prior period, (2) what's working / what isn't and why, (3) how the agency fee was deployed (ad spend, chatting, etc.). Document the no-fight exit policy explicitly — it costs nothing and statistically earns returns. Add these to Palm's creator onboarding agreement as stated commitments.

new_findings.topic: Psychological profile of high-spending fans (whale psychology)
new_findings.title: Core whale profile: isolated blue-collar/mid-income men aged 25–40 with disposable income and limited female access

variants.claim: The highest-spending OnlyFans fans share three overlapping psychological drivers: loneliness/isolation, attraction to someone perceived as out of their league, and dopamine from the gamified PPV unlock mechanic.
variants.what_they_do: Based on running thousands of concurrent fan conversations, he identifies the primary demographic as blue-collar or mid-income men (truck drivers, ex-military, some tech/finance) aged 25–40, with disposable income but limited social and romantic access. He maps three compounding psychological levers: (1) loneliness solved by parasocial intimacy, (2) willingness to spend freely on someone perceived as unattainable, (3) gambling-like dopamine from the grayed-out PPV unlock. He uses this model to train chatters on sequencing: build the parasocial bond, anchor perceived unattainability, then introduce the PPV unlock at the peak of anticipation.

sources.quote: largely these people are relatively lonely, right? And so this is the quickest solution. On top of that, they're speaking to someone that's hypothetically completely out of their league
the third component is that gamification aspect of it... the video that you send when you lock it for a specific price is sort of grayed out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9 partial) but no documented psychological model of fan motivation to guide chatter training or PPV sequencing. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat SOP and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate this three-driver psychological model (loneliness, out-of-league perception, gamification) into Palm's future chatter training curriculum. Map each driver to a specific chat tactic: loneliness → regular personalized check-ins and remembering details; out-of-league → creator framing and selective availability; gamification → PPV unlock pacing with teaser descriptions. Also consider adding fan psychological segmentation fields to the Fan Tracker CRM to help identify which lever is dominant per fan.

new_findings.title: Optimize acquisition for ROI/LTV, not cost-per-lead

variants.claim: Acquisition should be optimized on return-on-spend against creator lifetime value, not cost-per-lead, which lets you profitably pay far more for high-quality leads.
variants.what_they_do: Tracks average creator retention and revenue to derive LTV; with a 5k LTV avatar, a 500 dollar cost-per-lead beats a 100 dollar lead with low LTV. Once 100+ avatar leads are captured, builds 1% lookalike audiences to compound quality.

sources.quote: the focus metric always is ROI... cost per lead is a bad metric and doesn't really mean anything
have the balls to do that if you know that the average LTV is 5K, 10K, 20K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm computes weekly invoices but has no documented LTV, retention-rate, or acquisition unit-economics tracking (§7 THIN, §9 PARTIAL).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Instrument creator LTV (avg tenure x avg monthly Palm revenue) and retention so any future paid acquisition is judged on ROI, and so the data feeds pricing and retention decisions too.

new_findings.title: Install a system to capture proof continuously

variants.claim: Agencies should run a standing mechanism that captures every creator win, screenshot, and testimonial as it happens, since proof fuels both acquisition and trust.
variants.what_they_do: Routes every positive creator message, analytics screenshot, and voice note into a Drive/table immediately, so each win can later be turned into an ad or case study; recommends installing this even before running ads.

sources.quote: start installing the systems to capture proof like immediately
you need to like start having a system within your agency that captures proof

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm collects fan/whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but has no documented system for capturing creator-facing wins/testimonials as marketing proof (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a lightweight proof-capture pipeline (Airtable table or Drive) that logs creator wins and testimonials, feeding both the acquisition funnel and a credibility library.

new_findings.title: Personal/agency brand is the real acquisition constraint

variants.claim: The binding constraint on agency growth is obscurity, not lead supply, so building a known brand is the highest-leverage acquisition asset.
variants.what_they_do: Argues all growth is constrained by irrelevance; a brand compounds trust before the first conversation so a large share of pipeline becomes inbound, and reframes competition as obscurity rather than other agencies. Notes ~7M creators means the market is nowhere near saturated.

sources.quote: your real constraint isn't leads. It's irrelevance. Nobody knows you exist
80% of my pipeline is from inbound, from my brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no agency/founder brand or content presence for acquisition (§6 THIN); every Josh DM starts from zero trust.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Have Josh (or a Palm-brand channel) publish consistent niche authority content so inbound trust precedes outreach, reducing reliance on cold DMs.

new_findings.title: Volume, clarity, and proof drive content performance

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social engine is data-driven on what to film (§1 MATURE) but cadence/frequency is not codified (§1 GAP) and messaging clarity/proof are not formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a high-volume posting cadence in the SMM hub (§11) and add avatar-language and proof guidelines to the content pipeline, complementing Palm's inspo-driven ideation.

new_findings.title: Delegate menial work to protect creative output

variants.claim: Founders should delegate menial, repeatable work (posting, editing, thumbnails) early to keep the creative/strategic brain free for high-leverage output.
variants.what_they_do: Delegates short-form editing, posting, and other menial tasks while keeping idea-capture and filming in-house; recommends building a light team (editors, thumbnail/caption help) early to avoid burnout from doing it all.

sources.quote: you want to start delegating your editing, your anything that's like menial like posting
burning out from doing it all yourself. That's a very other common pitfall

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already delegates manual posting to a contractor (Amin, §5) and editing to editors (§1), but has no formalized delegation/hiring plan (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize a delegation ladder (what the founder keeps vs hands off) and hiring criteria, building on Palm's existing posting/editing outsourcing.

new_findings.title: Cold outreach is a volume-consistency-conversation game

variants.claim: Cold outreach signs creators predictably only when run as a system of high volume, long consistency, and value-first conversation rather than clever one-off scripts.
variants.what_they_do: Commits to 100 personalized DMs/day for 100 days (25/account across 4 accounts), value-first openers via voice/video note, and finding the avatar through conversation (10-30% qualify) rather than pre-filtering on the profile; built a 500k/mo agency on cold outreach before running a single ad.

sources.quote: the real unit of measurement is 100 personalized DMs a day for 100 days
voice and video cut through 10 times better than text

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led but not run as a tracked, committed-volume system (§6 THIN); no defined daily quota or multi-account structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalize Palm outbound as a committed daily-volume system (per-day DM quota, multi-account, voice/video openers, qualify-in-conversation) rather than ad-hoc DMs.

new_findings.title: Use proven evergreen content verticals, not niches/trends

variants.claim: Standardized evergreen content verticals built on the creator's own personality outperform niche-ing or chasing trends.
variants.what_they_do: Plugs every creator into the same proven verticals (e.g. Omegle, talking-head) rather than gym/office/etc niches, posting ~6x/day on IG and ~20x/day on trial reels across 1-2 accounts each; argues 'you are your own niche' and that conversion density (welcome msg, funnel, conversion posts) matters more than raw views.

sources.quote: We have scientifically engineered specific verticals that work with every avatar fit creator
you are your own niche as a creator

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm decides what to film from viral inspo data (§1 MATURE) but does not codify reusable evergreen verticals or a per-day posting cadence (§1 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a small set of evergreen, personality-led verticals and a high daily posting cadence in the SMM hub, layered on top of Palm's existing inspo-driven ideation.

new_findings.title: Replace yourself via ownership ladder, not task delegation

variants.claim: Founders scale out of the business by delegating outcomes and decision-making (an ownership ladder), not by dumping tasks, which only deepens dependency.
variants.what_they_do: Audits dollar-value-per-hour (only hires when a low-DVPH task blocks a high one), develops people up a level 1-5 accountability dial from doer to owner, and installs a decision filter so nobody escalates a problem without bullets + 2-3 solutions + a recommendation; uses the '30-day disappear test' to find the ceiling.

sources.quote: delegation actually works is if you delegate outcomes plus decision-making instead of delegating tasks
never bring me a problem without what happened... 2-3 solutions, your recommendation

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's org is thin (§10 GAP): no org chart, ownership model, or decision framework; founder/tech concentrated on Josh and Evan.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ownership-ladder + decision-filter operating model so Palm functions (acquisition, content, chat, ops) each have an owner and decisions don't route through the founders — directly feeds the teammate-org initiative.

new_findings.title: Source creators from OF-video comment sections and push to a call

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm acquisition is outbound-DM + referral but with no documented sourcing method, message template, or call-booking step (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a sourcing playbook (comment-section mining of help-seeking creators), a curiosity-led opener, multi-platform outreach, and call-booking as the CTA.

new_findings.title: Build a fan profile through scripted questions before any PPV

variants.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
variants.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.

sources.quote: what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but no chatting scripts or data-capture SOP (§8 OUTSOURCED).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pair Palm's fan CRM with a scripted data-collection intake and a build-up PPV ladder for the in-house/AI chat build.

new_findings.title: Hire cheap offshore chatters but train them intensively and systemize the work

variants.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
variants.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.

sources.quote: Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatters yet (§8) and no staffing/training model documented (§10 THIN); this informs the future in-house chat team's hiring and training design.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Plan Palm's in-house chat team around systemized scripts + intensive recurring training rather than relying on hires' prior experience.

new_findings.topic: systemize then scale one creator at a time
new_findings.title: Systemize day-one onboarding and perfect one creator before scaling

variants.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
variants.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.

sources.quote: It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is multi-creator past this stage, but the 'systemize before you scale' discipline maps onto Palm's THIN/GAP areas (acquisition §6, pricing §7, retention §9) that aren't yet documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply 'document and systemize before delegating/scaling' to close Palm's THIN areas before further growth.

new_findings.title: Use an Intrigue-Curiosity-CTA opener and book the call fast

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented DM script, send-rate discipline, or follow-up cadence for acquisition (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ICC opener template, a manual-typed/personalized/spaced sending SOP to avoid bans, and a 2-week daily follow-up rule; track per-step conversion.

new_findings.title: Qualify creators against a checklist and disqualify on red flags

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

sources.quote: Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding captures admin fields but has no documented fit scorecard or red-flag screen (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an ideal-client checklist plus a red-flag screen to the onboarding/intake step so Palm signs for fit and retention, not just availability.

new_findings.title: Anchor on a 50% full-service split, don't undersell at 20-30%

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale/tiers (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's commission rationale with a 50% default anchor and explicit guidance on when to deviate, so splits aren't negotiated ad hoc.

new_findings.title: Sell content as an escalating PPV script ladder

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

sources.quote: the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 GAP) and is thin on OF-vault/PPV content (§2); chatting is outsourced (§8) so the sequence is build-toward for in-house/AI chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture a default PPV script ladder (stage prices + free-content-between-stages tactic) as a starting pricing position and as build-toward content for the AI-chat project.

new_findings.title: Spin up backup/secondary accounts and post across many platforms

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution strategy (Reddit, multi-account, S4S) is an explicit GAP (§5); cadence is partially uncodified (§1). Note: this overlaps Palm's AI warmup multi-account capability (§3/§4).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-account/multi-platform traffic strategy with tracking links for attribution and a week-ahead scheduling cadence; reuse AI-warmup account infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Onboard with a documented same-day process and a welcome video

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding is a form wizard → Creators table (§6); strong on contract automation but no documented expectation-setting welcome video or same-day activation SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a standardized welcome video + same-day activation checklist to onboarding to set expectations and cut early churn.

new_findings.title: Run three departments (acquisition, marketing, chatting) each under a manager

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

sources.quote: you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm org is THIN (§10): no management layer between founders and ICs, no org chart.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a department-and-manager org (acquisition / marketing / chatting) with the founder managing through managers as Palm scales past the current flat structure.

new_findings.title: Do every role yourself first, document it, then hand it off

variants.claim: Learn each part of the business personally and turn it into an SOP before delegating, so hires run a documented process.
variants.what_they_do: Owner did outreach/closing/onboarding/marketing/chatting himself first, then wrote an SOP doc + Loom video for each task so new hires self-train from the document instead of hours of live training. Hires for hunger/reliability/coachability over experience (experienced people resist the system). Tracks KPIs on every function (DMs sent, replies, calls booked, clients closed, revenue per chatter).

sources.quote: you funnel all of that traffic over to Only Fans. And then the second thing is the chatting.
One of the first things that you need to do is connect their Only Fans account to your Inflow.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is strong on productized content tooling (§1) but has no stated document-before-delegate discipline; team/org THIN (§10).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize SOP + Loom documentation per role before backfilling it, hire for coachability, and attach KPIs to each function.

new_findings.title: Put AI creators on Fanvue and make every asset look real

variants.claim: AI creators can't use OnlyFans, so run them on an AI-friendly platform like Fanvue and prioritize realistic presentation over obvious perfection.
variants.what_they_do: OnlyFans does not allow AI; Fanvue welcomes AI, lets you label the account AI, and takes 15% vs OF's 20%. Build the persona like Build-A-Bear (face to niche, then body), batch content for both social and the paid platform, make the IG bio/handle look like a real person (uni, city, age), then funnel reels traffic to Fanvue and monetize via subscription + tips + PPV. Same can scale to ~$3-8k/mo on one model with no revenue split.

sources.quote: Only Fans does not allow AI models. It takes 20% fees... Fan View... they advocate AI creators. They take 15%.
The more realistic you make it, the better your results will be... bio that maybe says which university they're in... which city that they live in.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI personas currently funnel to OnlyFans via social (§3), distinct from the Fanvue AI-creator model; Palm's recreation/warmup pipeline (§3/§4) is well-suited to this future line (build-toward).
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm launches true AI creators (can't pass OF verification), plan a Fanvue home with AI labeling and realistic profiles, reusing the existing recreation + warmup pipeline; keep the real-creator→OF line separate.

new_findings.title: Weigh AI's zero-follower cold start and believability problem before betting on it

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs hardware-isolated AI warmup + EU AI Act labeling (§3/§4), which directly mitigates the cold-start/believability risk this raises; useful as a counter-view weighting Palm's real-first core (§0).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep real creators as the core and treat AI as a hedge; lean on Palm's warmup/labeling stack to de-risk the cold-start and believability problems this guru flags before scaling an AI line.

new_findings.title: Use the fan's name, kill mass-messages

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

sources.quote: people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but personalization data and tone live with outsourced chatters (§8). Build-toward plus owning the data.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Ensure Palm's own per-fan CRM captures the name and interests, and bar generic mass-message bundle blasts, so personalization survives any chatting-vendor change and feeds future AI chat.

new_findings.topic: Revenue vs profit distinction at scale
new_findings.title: High revenue means nothing without margin — $100k revenue can yield $3k profit

variants.claim: Agencies can hit $100,000/month in revenue while netting as little as $3,000 in profit, so raw revenue targets are misleading without tracking margin.
variants.what_they_do: TDM explicitly warns against 'brute forcing' to $50–70k/month and treating it as success; they frame margin as the true health metric, arguing that low-margin agencies cannot reinvest in better team, systems, or experimentation, which caps growth.

sources.quote: some agencies will do $100,000 in revenue and $3,000 in profit

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented internal baseline for margin targets or profit-vs-revenue tracking (§7 GAP). Weekly invoices are auto-computed but there is no documented P&L framework or margin floor that triggers a scaling decision.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a minimum margin % threshold (e.g., 30–40% net) as an internal KPI alongside gross revenue. Build a simple P&L view into the admin portal or Airtable so Josh and Evan can see whether revenue growth is actually profitable before committing to new hires or infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Take a week off at $100k/month to expose founder dependency before it becomes a ceiling

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is early-stage with Josh leading acquisition and strategy hands-on (§6). No documented test for founder dependency exists. Given the current scale, this is a forward-looking tool, but the diagnostic framework is immediately useful to plan against.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Schedule a planned 5-day founder-absence drill (even a partial one — Josh steps back from one department at a time) to surface which processes break. Use the results to prioritize which workflows need SOPs or a designated owner first. Document the gaps in Airtable.

new_findings.title: Tie each team role's compensation to the specific business metric that role drives

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

sources.quote: media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team structure is thin (§10): Amin is a contractor, editors exist, and an external chat team is outsourced. No documented incentive structure or performance-linked comp bands exist. This is a GAP as Palm begins building in-house teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When hiring the first in-house chatters (§8 roadmap) and any additional editors or marketing staff, design role-specific KPIs with a variable comp component tied to those KPIs (e.g., chatters earn a bonus % of PPV revenue above a baseline; editors earn a bonus per post that hits a reach threshold). Document comp bands in the org structure build-out.

new_findings.topic: Incremental hiring in step with revenue milestones
new_findings.title: Add team members and build systems in proportion to revenue growth — not all at once

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

sources.quote: 10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring and org structure are thin and undocumented (§10 GAP). There is no scaling roadmap that ties headcount or system builds to specific revenue milestones. Palm risks the 'brute force' trap the video warns against if growth outpaces internal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a simple revenue-milestone roadmap in Airtable or Notion: at each $X increment in monthly revenue, define the next hire, SOP, or system to be built. Even a rough version (e.g., at $X add a second editor; at $Y formalize creator KPI reporting; at $Z begin in-house chat hiring) provides the incremental discipline TDM describes.

new_findings.topic: Content repurposing — structural uniqueness to evade IG detection
new_findings.title: Make repurposed Reels structurally different, not just color-adjusted

variants.claim: Changing only brightness/contrast is insufficient to fool Instagram's duplicate-detection; the video's structure must be genuinely altered.
variants.what_they_do: They download the original Reel watermark-free (SnapTick/SnapInsta), then apply structural edits in any editing app: trim 0.1s from a scene cut, mirror the clip, insert a different opening scene, and also adjust color/contrast slightly — the goal is a video that IS different, not merely looks different.

sources.quote: you're going to take 0.1 second out of one scene. You're going to basically mirror the video, right? You're going to add a different scene at the beginning

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) produces organic, creator-filmed Reels and does not currently have a repurposing workflow. The structural-edit technique is a distinct add-on to the existing pipeline for any scaled reposting layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm ever runs multi-account reposting for a single creator, document a mandatory structural-edit checklist (scene trim, mirror, new intro scene + minor color grade) before export. Add this as a step in the existing pipeline for repurposed variants, distinct from the organic primary post.

new_findings.topic: Multi-account content rotation — avoiding identical Reels grids
new_findings.title: Rotate which repurposed video goes to which account — never mirror the same grid

variants.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
variants.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.

sources.quote: if you have three different videos per day… you're going to post all three of them on the main account. One of them on the first reposting account, one of them on the second, and one of them on the third

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current scheduling system (§5) routes posts via Publer or Telegram/Amin but is designed for one primary account per creator. There is no documented logic for distributing content across a cluster of repurposing accounts for a single creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a distribution-rotation rule in the scheduling layer: for any creator running N repurposing accounts, assign each daily video to a unique subset of those accounts via a round-robin or modular assignment in Airtable/Publer. This prevents grid mirroring and reduces ban risk.

new_findings.topic: Organic vs. repurposed account architecture — strict separation
new_findings.title: Never mix organic and repurposed content on the same IG account

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

sources.quote: you split up into both teams because you have your main chick and you have your side chicks… Never mix the two. Never.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) is entirely organic/creator-filmed for the primary account. Palm does not currently run a repurposing-account layer. This finding clarifies the correct architecture IF Palm adds one: keep the existing organic pipeline untouched and stand up a separate repurposing operation alongside it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm scales to multi-account reposting, codify the two-tier rule in the SMM hub redesign (§11): tag accounts as 'organic-primary' or 'repurpose-only' in Airtable, and enforce via posting-workflow routing so editors never schedule repurposed content to an organic-primary account.

new_findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale, floor, or tier model (§7 GAP). The commission field exists in the Creators table but the logic behind what percentage to set is unwritten.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

new_findings.title: Start organic on Twitter, TikTok, IG only — add Reddit/bots later
