sources.quote: you have no tracking mechanism — you're probably not using tracking links to see where the traffic's coming from, and every single creator is getting handled in a different way

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's OF vault/PPV backend is THIN (§2) with no documented standardisation of per-creator page management or tracking links. Chat is outsourced (§8) which partially insulates Palm from the chaos scenario, but the lack of standardised backend setup means scaling a creator's traffic still risks operational breakdown.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Before aggressively scaling any creator's traffic, complete a backend readiness checklist: (1) tracking links live per platform, (2) PPV content plan drafted, (3) chatter capacity confirmed with external team, (4) escalation SOP defined. This is also foundational groundwork for the in-house chat roadmap (§8 build-toward).

new_findings.title: Write the SOP before hiring, not after — every role must have a documented replacement path

variants.claim: Without an SOP for every role, a new hire requires weeks of founder handholding, negating the time savings of hiring and blocking agency growth.
variants.what_they_do: Ellis and his partner documented every job into SOPs before delegating. They reinvested 100% of revenue for the first 12 months into the next hire, next system, or software — taking no personal salary. The founder's job is specifically to build the SOP so the hire can self-onboard and replace the founder in that function.

sources.quote: if you do not have an SOP for every single part of your business, every single job, every single role, then again, you're never going to make it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team/org structure is THIN (§10) with no documented org chart, hiring scorecards, or comp bands. Josh currently handles acquisition personally (§6), which Ellis explicitly identifies as a founder bottleneck to eliminate first.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Prioritise SOP creation for the two highest-founder-time functions (acquisition outreach and creator onboarding). Write the SOP while doing the job, then hire against it. Model Ellis's reinvestment discipline: define a minimum reinvestment rate of revenue back into team/systems before founder draw, especially during the $30K–$100K scaling phase.

new_findings.title: Send blurred/cropped preview before full PPV to spark curiosity and drive unlock

variants.claim: Sending a blurred or cropped content preview instead of the full asset creates curiosity that converts fans to purchase the unlock.
variants.what_they_do: Rather than describing content verbally or sending it outright, chatters send a teaser (blurred image or cropped clip) accompanied by a curiosity-hook line such as 'guess what's under this honey, unlock it to find out.' The preview functions as a visual purchase prompt.

sources.quote: use teasers and previews instead of sending the full content immediately send a blurred or cropped preview to spark curiosity

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV sequencing SOP (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is a concrete mechanical step missing from any documented Palm chatting or PPV workflow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the blur/crop preview as a standard step in the PPV send sequence for both the outsourced chat team (share as guidance) and future in-house SOP. Editors/content team should produce a 'teaser variant' of each PPV asset at upload time to make this operationally easy.

new_findings.title: Triage multi-account chat queues by subscriber spend history, not recency

variants.claim: When managing multiple accounts simultaneously, chatters should prioritize responses to high-spending or long-tenure subscribers over the general queue.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters explicitly rank their open conversations: whales and loyal long-term members get first responses. Low-spend or new subscribers are handled after, often with templated messages to maintain efficiency without abandoning them.

sources.quote: prioritize high value chats focus on subscribers who frequently spend money or longtime members

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Whale-hunting module and Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but no documented protocol for chat prioritization within the outsourced team. This is a logical extension of the whale-hunting data Palm already collects.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Export whale/high-spend fan flags from Palm's Fan Tracker into whatever interface the chat team uses, and formalize a 'respond to these first' rule. This bridges the existing whale-hunting infrastructure (§9) with chat execution — a quick operational win.

new_findings.title: Chatters maintain per-fan notes (age, relationship status, interests) to personalize every session

variants.claim: Chatters who record personal details shared by fans during conversations can use those details in future sessions to create a sense of genuine ongoing relationship.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to jot down fan-disclosed details — name, age, relationship status, hobbies, personal anecdotes — and reference them in later messages. The explicit goal is to make the fan feel 'seen, heard, and valued' rather than like one of many subscribers.

sources.quote: jot down details about them you want to know more than just their name keep track of things like their age relationship status and any personal tibbits they share

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but it is oriented toward spend/whale detection rather than personal-detail logging for chat personalization. The outsourced team's note-taking practices are unknown.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Fan Tracker with a free-text 'fan notes' field (personal details, interests, shared anecdotes) that chatters can read before and write to after each session. This aligns with Palm's existing CRM infrastructure and directly improves re-engagement quality.

new_findings.title: Track which message templates drive the most sales and circulate winners across the team

variants.claim: Systematically monitoring which specific messages generate the most engagement and sales, then sharing winners as team templates, compounds chatter performance over time.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to set daily sales targets, track which message styles and scripts convert best, and collaborate with teammates to share high-performing templates. If a flirty line is working across multiple fans, it gets codified as a reusable template for the whole team.

sources.quote: if a certain flirty message is working well for multiple subscribers you can use it as a template for future chats

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat performance tracking or template iteration loop (§8 OUTSOURCED). The outsourced team is a black box for this kind of feedback. Palm's content pipeline (§1) does this well for social content (viral scoring, promote-best) but the same logic has never been applied to chat.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply Palm's existing 'scrape → analyze → promote best → reuse' logic from the content pipeline (§1) to chat scripts: have the outsourced team (and eventually in-house) log top-converting messages weekly, review them in a shared doc, and build a living script library. This is a direct structural parallel to a system Palm already operates well.

new_findings.title: Performance-based confidence: good results eliminate creator flight risk better than financial lock-in

variants.claim: If an agency is doing a genuinely good job for a creator, the creator won't withhold payment or leave, because abandoning the agency mid-month costs her more money than she'd gain.
variants.what_they_do: Bjorn's argument: the agency's leverage isn't financial control (bank swap) but value delivery. A creator who is earning significantly more with the agency than she would alone won't risk that income by stiffing the agency. He uses this logic to justify switching all clients to invoicing — the quality of results is the retention mechanism.

sources.quote: if you are that worried then you are doing a shitty job because if you were doing a really good job for the model then you can have confidence that they're not going to go anywhere

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented retention or performance-success cadence for creators (§9 gap). The implicit logic matches — Palm presumably aims to deliver results — but there's no written framework for measuring 'are we doing a good enough job that this creator won't leave?' or communicating that value back to the creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a simple creator-facing performance report (monthly or weekly): earnings this period, earnings trend, key milestones. Sending this proactively before invoicing reinforces the value Palm delivers and makes the invoice feel deserved. This directly supports payment security without any financial lock-in mechanics. Ties into §9 retention gap.

new_findings.topic: Traffic source tracking links per platform
new_findings.title: Use Per-Platform Tracking Links to Attribute OF Revenue to Traffic Sources

variants.claim: Using a dedicated tracking link for each traffic source (e.g., one link for Twitter) lets you measure exactly how much OF revenue each platform generates.
variants.what_they_do: Each traffic channel (X account, IG, promo page, etc.) gets its own tracking/inflow link. Griffin shows a single Twitter tracking link that generated $186K in OF revenue, proving channel-level attribution. This enables decisions like 'Twitter is responsible for ~40–50% of this creator's revenue growth' rather than guessing.

sources.quote: This is a tracking link in inflow from one of our Twitter accounts on our creator using this strategy and we've generated $186,000 through this link.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses tracking links (mentioned in the video's own intro showing Palm-style data). However, Palm's traffic strategy documentation is a GAP (§5) — it's unclear whether Palm systematically assigns per-platform tracking links for all creators and all channels, or whether this is ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize per-platform OF tracking links as a mandatory step in creator setup. For every active traffic channel (IG bio, X main account, Reddit, promo pages), create a unique tracking link at onboarding. Store these in the Creators table in Airtable with a 'Traffic Source Links' field. Pull revenue-by-source into the whale-hunting / fan intelligence dashboard (§9) for channel-level ROI reporting.

new_findings.title: Hire for coachability and reliability, not pre-existing chatting skill

variants.claim: The correct chatter hiring filter is competence, reliability, and honesty — not existing chatting skill — because a motivated person can be trained to top-level in 2–3 weeks.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly de-emphasize prior chatting experience as a hiring criterion. Instead prioritize candidates who demonstrate effort, follow-through, and personal motivation throughout the screening pipeline. Agency trains chatters to top level within 2–3 weeks once the right person is selected. The entire multi-stage funnel (attention checks, situational tests, live calls, Loom compliance) is designed to surface coachable, reliable people — not to find pre-trained chatters.

sources.quote: It's about you hiring competent, reliable, and honest people that you can teach the skill of chatting. Because we taught chatters in like two or three weeks to get to a top level.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter hiring criteria (§8 outsourced). This philosophy should anchor Palm's future hiring rubric — it means Palm can source from a much wider talent pool and doesn't need to pay a premium for 'experienced' chatters.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document 'coachability, reliability, honesty' as Palm's three core chatter hiring criteria. Build the scoring rubric for each screening stage around these three traits rather than chatting knowledge. This also reduces cost — experienced chatters command higher pay; trainable reliable people do not.

new_findings.topic: PPV content description honesty as retention lever
new_findings.title: Describe PPV content accurately to reduce buyer's remorse and sustain spend

variants.claim: Telling fans exactly what a locked video contains — rather than implying they'll 'finally see everything' — lets the fan self-qualify, reduces regret, and keeps them spending longer.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to describe PPV content as accurately and enticingly as possible without lying about what's actually shown. For non-nude creators especially, they never imply the fan is about to see explicit content they won't actually get. They still use persuasion and soft negotiation, but the content description itself is honest.

sources.quote: we tell them 'this is what you're going to see in this video' and I think that's a really important component where he can make his own decision on whether he's going to purchase it or not

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatting SOP exists at Palm (§8 outsourced). This is a specific scriptable rule for the in-house chat build-toward: a 'PPV description standard' that distinguishes persuasive framing from factual misrepresentation.
palm_comparison.recommendation: In Palm's future chatter training, create a PPV description template that separates (a) accurate content summary from (b) persuasive framing/emotional hook. Teach chatters the distinction explicitly. This reduces refund requests and extends fan LTV, particularly for real creators who don't produce explicit content.

new_findings.title: Six-step ad script for qualified creator leads

variants.claim: A repeatable six-step ad script (attention, key benefit, without-the-pain, proof, time-sensitive offer, downside of inaction) structures creative for conversion rather than ad-hoc messaging.
variants.what_they_do: Uses a fixed 6-step structure with one chosen angle (speed, risk-reduction, or ease); language must be coherent from ad to VSL to funnel to call and matched to a specific avatar (e.g. creators already doing 10k/mo) to avoid attracting low-quality opportunity-seekers.

sources.quote: the six-step ad script looks like this... number one, attention... number six is the downside of inaction

palm_comparison.vs_us: No acquisition messaging framework or avatar definition exists at Palm (§6 THIN).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt the 6-step script and define a concrete creator avatar (revenue band, follower count) so Palm outreach and any future content speaks one coherent, qualifying message.

new_findings.title: Real creator + AI clone, kept on OnlyFans

variants.claim: Cloning a real signed creator into an AI model and mixing AI with real content lets an agency keep OnlyFans conversion while multiplying volume.
variants.what_they_do: Take 20-30 photos of a real signed creator, train an AI model on her face/body, generate motion-control videos, then post a real+AI mix that all funnels to her real OnlyFans (no AI sold past the wall). One model films 35-40 videos/week; AI fills the other 40-45, enabling 4+ accounts.

sources.quote: you take a real creator and you generate your own AI profile of that creator

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already runs a recreation pipeline for AI personas funneling to OnlyFans (§3), but does NOT yet clone REAL creators to scale their social volume. This is a direct extension of Palm's existing AI-persona capability onto the real-creator core business (§0/§1).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot cloning one cooperative real creator: train an AI likeness, post a real/AI mix to her existing funnel, keep all OF vault content 100% real. Leverage Palm's existing recreation pipeline.

new_findings.title: Hybrid keeps OnlyFans trust vs full-AI Fanvue conversion loss

variants.claim: Full-AI creators are forced onto less-trusted third-party platforms where conversion drops 30-50% versus OnlyFans, making hybrid (real OF) structurally more profitable.
variants.what_they_do: Argues full AI cannot use OnlyFans (ID verification, no AI content allowed) so they go to Fansly/Fanvue/Fanfix where saved-credit-card trust is absent and conversion tanks; hybrid keeps everything on OnlyFans.

sources.quote: Your conversion rates will literally drop 30 to 50% just by doing that platform rather than Only Fans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Confirms Palm's core OnlyFans funnel as the high-conversion asset (§0). Palm's AI personas use OnlyFans-via-social, so this validates Palm's choice not to go Fanvue-only.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Hold the OnlyFans funnel as the conversion anchor for both real and AI lines; treat Fanvue-style platforms only as fallback, never primary.

new_findings.title: Strip AI metadata before posting or reach is throttled

variants.claim: AI-generated media carries hidden metadata that platforms read to tag/throttle content, so removing it before posting materially restores reach.
variants.what_they_do: Removes AI metadata from generated images/videos with a free tool or re-export before posting; reports reach went up ~40% and videos hit explore again after they started doing this.

sources.quote: there's a metadata attached to those files that says AI

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's AI-persona program is built for compliance (EU AI Act label + watermark, §3), which is the opposite posture. This is a competitor risk tactic Palm should note but weigh against its compliance stance.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Note as a reach lever for non-EU/unlabeled experiments only; do NOT apply to Palm's EU-AI-Act-labeled personas. Flag the compliance tension for leadership.

new_findings.title: Engagement-trap, trendjack, and paid promo pages on X

variants.claim: X growth is driven by reply-baiting engagement traps, fast trendjacking, and paying large promo pages to seed content with mystery hooks.
variants.what_they_do: Posts reply-bait ('describe me in one word'), jumps trends like Grok/Epstein image gimmicks within 15-30 min, and pays promo pages (100-500k followers) $50-200/post that hook viewers into clicking through to the creator's pinned reply.

sources.quote: the content designed to get replies

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's traffic strategy is undocumented (§5 gap). These are specific paid + organic X tactics with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture as build-out menu for an X traffic SOP: engagement-trap caption library, daily trend monitoring, and a tested promo-page budget per creator.

new_findings.title: Girlfriend-experience parasocial selling, not PPV spam

variants.claim: The girlfriend experience (parasocial connection, scripted live-feeling sells, no PPV spam) drives the highest and most durable chatting revenue.
variants.what_they_do: Optimizes the OF wall to feel like 'Snapchatting,' uses 7-10 stage scripts with verification images, never initiates sexual content (lets the fan initiate), handles broke fans with ego-protecting lines, and forbids PPV spamming across the whole team.

sources.quote: the GF experience, the only thing that I try to run in OFM

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is OUTSOURCED at Palm with no in-house SOP (§8). This is direct build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house and AI chat teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the GFE framework (wall optimization, staged scripts, verification, no-spam rule) as the seed SOP for Palm's future in-house/AI chatting.

new_findings.title: Loom-video SOPs to eliminate training misinterpretation

variants.claim: Recording yourself doing each task as a Loom video (rather than written docs) is the most reliable way to train hires and remove misinterpretation.
variants.what_they_do: Films a Loom once per task and reuses it forever; pairs Loom with backup documents for objections; uses live chatter monitoring on first 1-2 shifts plus 'trust but verify' checks for the first 1-2 weeks.

sources.quote: Loom recordings

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has SOP-style workflows in the editor/warmup UIs but no documented Loom-first training standard for new hires (§10). Cheap, high-leverage practice.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize Loom-video SOPs for every Palm role (editor, poster, warmup operator, future chatters); store alongside the existing playbook editor.

new_findings.title: Branded persona replacing pure volume in 2026

variants.claim: A distinct creator persona (identity, consistent theme, story) is replacing raw posting volume as the driver of growth, conversion, and ban-resistance.
variants.what_they_do: Defines a brand as persona + consistent theme + story (e.g. dominant/funny/confident); reports a branded creator going 2k to 50k/mo in two months, and writes a one-sentence persona applied to bios, captions, content, and chats.

sources.quote: make it that her persona

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline optimizes for viral content ideation (§1) but persona/branding is not a codified input. This argues brand should be a first-class field upstream of content selection.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a defined persona/brand brief to creator onboarding and feed it into the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas reinforce a consistent identity.

new_findings.title: Hybrid AI makes salary creators highly profitable

variants.claim: Pairing a low-cost salary creator with AI volume yields outsized margins because output is decoupled from the creator's motivation or filming.
variants.what_they_do: Cites a student paying salary creators ~$1.8-2k/mo each, generating ~$15-20k/mo per creator via hybrid AI volume, keeping the bulk as profit since the salary model only needs to produce NSFW vault content.

sources.quote: he's paying 6K to make over $45,000

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented pricing/commission MODEL or salary-vs-split rationale (§7 gap). This is a concrete alternative deal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Evaluate a salary-creator + AI-volume deal structure as an alternative to revenue split for select creators; document the margin math in a pricing model.

new_findings.title: Snapchat as an under-saturated OF traffic + income channel

variants.claim: Snapchat is an under-exploited OFM channel that simultaneously drives OF subs, cross-promotes other platforms, and pays direct creator monetization.
variants.what_they_do: Sets up a real-looking account, posts ~5 spotlights/day plus 30-100 clickbait story snaps/day (repurposed via repurpose.io or a VA), CTAs only once a week to avoid disabling, and uses paid promoted-snap boosts; cites a creator earning as much from Snapchat as from Instagram.

sources.quote: boost your initial following

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution is IG/TikTok via Publer + Telegram (§5); Snapchat is absent from Palm's stack. New channel with no Palm baseline.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Pilot Snapchat for one high-output creator: repurpose existing content, weekly-only CTA, test promoted snaps; measure as a third revenue lever.

new_findings.title: Provision reserve accounts for burn

variants.claim: Assuming a baseline account-ban burn rate and pre-provisioning reserve accounts is required to run social acquisition at scale without interruption.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media issues 200 accounts per platform per model (about 100 active immediately, 100 in reserve), runs a daily script using public profile views to auto-detect bans, and accepts that even fully organic operations always carry some burn.

sources.quote: 100 that become active immediately when they start then 100 for reserve... there's always the risk of having some bans

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs hardware-isolated AI account warmup (§3/§4 MATURE) but has no documented reserve-account or burn-rate provisioning model. Gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a reserve-account buffer per AI persona plus an automated daily ban-detection check, sized to an expected burn rate, so a ban never stalls a creator's funnel.

new_findings.title: Build so the founder can disappear

variants.claim: Heavy supervision layers and clear workflows let the founder step away while the business keeps running, which is the real test of a scaled agency.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media (300 employees, 60 models) layers supervisors into category managers into shift/market managers per department, and the founder says if he disappeared for six months the company would still run profitably.

sources.quote: if I disappear for 6 months right now, will my company grow? Maybe not exponentially, but it will run

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a thin org with many decisions through the founder and no org chart or scaling plan (§10 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a supervision layer (team leads / managers) per Palm function with explicit workflows so the founder can step out without the operation stalling.

new_findings.title: AI scores chat transcripts daily

variants.claim: An AI model trained on past chats can read every chatter's transcripts daily and surface buy-signals, missed offers, and coaching feedback that human supervisors cannot cover at scale.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media pulls Infloww message-board exports into an LLM trained on two years of their own chats to produce daily per-chatter reports (interest/buy signals vs offers sent, left-on-read counts, missed opportunities, KPIs vs peers) because even five supervisors cannot watch 30-40 chatters live.

sources.quote: an AI LLM model that we train... based on two years worth of data of our own chats... how many interest signals... opportunities they missed

palm_comparison.vs_us: Chatting is outsourced and Palm owns no QA loop (§8 OUTSOURCED), but AI chatting is an active Palm project. Direct build-toward for that roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: For Palm's AI-chatting project, design an AI transcript-QA layer that scores every conversation daily (buy-signal capture, offers sent, misses) and feeds coaching back to chatters.

new_findings.title: Give every model a written character

variants.claim: Building a written character/persona for each creator and aligning all content to it raises LTV by enabling a parasocial relationship rather than generic thirst traps.
variants.what_they_do: Hive Media sits each new model down to create an in-depth character sheet (occupation, personality, persona, backstory) and aligns outfits, scenes, and PPVs to it, deliberately targeting spending demographics by the model's look so chatters can monetize a fleshed-out character.

sources.quote: you're actually a character with a story and depth... build a better parasocial relationship with them

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline drives content from viral inspo data (§1) but there is no documented per-creator character/persona layer tying content to a sales story. Gap on branding/type-2.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a per-creator character sheet (persona, backstory, target fan demographic) that both content and the chat team work from, so content ladders into monetization.

new_findings.title: Ladder PPV prices upward in sequence

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

sources.quote: We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

palm_comparison.vs_us: No formal price ladder at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Publish a standard Palm PPV ladder with rising steps and trigger conditions, instead of single high asks.
